reason: Qusayy ibn Kilab unified scattered descendants and established control over Mecca and the Kaaba
narrative: Prior to Qusayy, Fihr's offspring lived in scattered, nomadic groups among their Kinana relatives. Qusayy gathered his kinsmen and took control of the Kaaba, establishing Quraysh as the dominant power in Mecca.

reason: Legendary migration from original homeland
narrative: According to legend, Rabi'a's original homelands were in the Tihamah region of western Arabia, from which they migrated northwards and eastwards

destination.regionId: Northwards and eastwards across Arabian Peninsula

reason: According to legend, moved after original inhabitants Tasm and Jadis were decimated by war
narrative: Legendary migration to al-Yamama from the Hejaz region

reason: Fled harsh rule of Banul Ukhaidhir dynasty
narrative: Moved to Wadi al-Allaqi near Aswan with women and children, worked in gold mining

reason: Seasonal work during pearling season
narrative: Traditional seasonal migration pattern - farming in the mountains during winter and working in pearling in coastal areas

origin.regionId: Ruus Al Jibal area of the Hajar Mountains

destination.regionId: Ras Al Khaimah villages (Sha'am and Rams)

reason: Settlement transition from nomadism
narrative: The Northern branch of Bani Qitab settled in the inland towns of Dhaid and Al Falayah

reason: Settlement and territorial separation
narrative: The Southern Bani Qitab, some 500 households, settled around the village of Aflaj Bani Qitab in the Dhahirah area. Over time these separated from the Northern section

reason: Seasonal work during pearling season
narrative: Traditionally, the Shihuh changed domicile, taking work in Ras Al Khaimah and Rams during the pearling season and farming in the Ruus Al Jibal area of the Hajar Mountains during the winter

reason: Unknown
narrative: Originally inhabited Wadi Qor area before dispersing to current locations in Ras Al Khaimah and Fujairah

destination.regionId: Ras Al Khaimah and Fujairah areas

reason: Tribal expansion and search for pastures
narrative: Early migration from ancestral homes near Medina to central Arabian territories

reason: Continued nomadic movement and raiding activities
narrative: Later migration to northern territories including the desert areas of southern Iraq and northern Saudi Arabia and Kuwait

destination.regionId: Northern Najd and Northern Bahrain (Eastern Arabia)

reason: Settlement and land cultivation
narrative: Early history suggests the Dhawahir originally populated the area of Al Dhahirah in present-day Oman before settling in the Buraimi Oasis area

reason: Population growth and limited territory
narrative: When the tribe enlarged and their area became narrow in Yemen, Najd and Hijaz, they gathered to decide their future. After rejecting conquering neighboring tribes, they chose to migrate north
They moved from Dumat Al-Jandal towards the Levant, eventually settling in the Karak region where they became numerous enough to outnumber the largest local tribes

reason: Under leadership of Sheikh Shafi bin Safar
narrative: Second migration towards the eastern village of Al Ahsa'a when their sheikh became Shafi bin Safar bin Mohammed Bin Shaba'an

reason: Early Islamic period migration
narrative: Connected to Miqdad ibn Aswad Al Kindi's immigration from Yemen to Oman during the time of Prophet Muhammad

reason: Settlement pattern establishment
narrative: By the turn of the 20th century, the Kunud were mostly settled on the East Coast, from Nizwa in Oman to Kalba in Sharjah and Al Hayl in Fujairah, as well as Mahdah and Buraimi

reason: Historical ties and proximity
narrative: Mahri established significant presence in northern Puntland due to historical connections and geographical proximity to Horn of Africa

reason: Voluntary amalgamation over centuries of different groups seeking to establish unified tribal identity and land claims
narrative: Critical research suggests the Manasir formed through voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous mostly Nubian groups, descendants of emigrating Arabs and recruited tribal minorities. Earlier travellers witnessed Beja and Bisharin influences from the Red Sea Hills among the Manasir.

origin.regionId: various origins (Red Sea Hills, Arab lands, local Nubian areas)

destination.regionId: Fourth Cataract of the Nile region

reason: Seasonal grazing for livestock during dry season
narrative: During the dry season some clans migrate to the desert area of the Kababish tribe to the west (Khala' Kabushiyah) others to the grazing grounds of Wad Hamid in the Ga'ali Country or to the Rubatab Country.

destination.regionId: Kababish territory, Ga'ali Country, Rubatab Country

reason: Settlement in area originally populated by Iranians
narrative: The Maraziq are believed to originally have migrated from Arabia, and to have taken the name of the district they settled in Rams, originally populated by Iranians.

reason: expansion and settlement
narrative: The Qahtan tribe settled in Al-Quwai'iyah region of Najd and expanded its influence in southern Najd, becoming one of the powerful tribes of the region for a period.

reason: return to original territory
narrative: After a period of prominence in Najd, the tribe eventually left and returned to its original home in the south.
Initial settlement in the Dhahirah and Sunaynah areas after emigrating to Eastern Arabia
Later migrations led some Al Bu Shamis to settle in Al Ain and the Buraimi Oasis

reason: Seasonal migration for date season work and pearl fishing
narrative: During warmer months, they migrated mainly to work during the date season and participated in pearl fishing season

reason: Settlement in oasis towns and coastal areas for pastoralism and pearl fishing
narrative: The Tunaij settled primarily in Dhaid (Sharjah), Rams (Ras Al Khaimah), and small numbers at Hamriyah, establishing both Bedouin centers inland and pearl fishing communities on the coast

reason: nomadic lifestyle and grazing patterns
narrative: Historically travelled as nomads covering up to 3,000 kilometres per year across the Arabian Peninsula, eventually settling in Qatar, Saudi Arabia, UAE, Kuwait and Bahrain

reason: Seeking new territories and trade opportunities
narrative: Early migration eastwards from Nejd to eastern Arabian Peninsula, where they established dominance over desert regions around Al-Hasa and Al-Qatif

reason: Acquisition of property and land
narrative: The Bani Yas later settled in and around Al Ain and Buraimi when the Al Bu Falah clan acquired property and land in the Buraimi Oasis

reason: Forced out by Himyarite expansion and pressure
narrative: By the 2nd century BC, Himyar was able to settle nomadic tribes and create a stronger nation, eventually incorporating Saba' and forcing Kahlan tribes out of the highlands

reason: Part of wave of immigration from Yemen bringing Malik bin Fahm immigrants north
narrative: The Arab element of the tribe is thought to be linked to the wave of immigration from Yemen in the second century

reason: Exodus from Yemen, invasion led by Usma bin Luai
narrative: The Tayy (later Shammar) migrated from Yemen and invaded the mountains of Ajā and Salma from Banu Assad and Banu Tamim in northern Arabia. These mountains were later renamed Jabal Shammar.

destination.regionId: Mountains of Ajā and Salma, northern Arabian Peninsula

reason: Following the dispersion of the Banu Azd from Yemen
narrative: Originally based in Yemen, the Banu Tayy migrated to northern Arabia in the late 2nd century CE, settling among the mountain ranges of Aja and Salma, which became known collectively as Jabal Tayy.

reason: Settlement and expansion under Malik ibn Fehm leadership
narrative: Many tribes descending from Zahran and Azd settled in Oman under leadership of Malik bin Fehm in the 3rd century, leading to the Arabization of Oman

reason: Political expansion and confederation building
narrative: Malik ibn Fehm established the Tanukh tribal confederation centered initially in ancient Al-Hira

reason: collapse of the Ma'rib Dam making living conditions unfavorable
narrative: According to traditional medieval Yemeni folklore, the Azd tribal group originally lived in Yemen, until the collapse of the Ma'rib Dam when they began emigrating to other parts of the Arabian Peninsula

reason: tribal expansion and settlement
narrative: Imran bin Amr and the bulk of the tribe went to Oman, where they established the Azdi presence in Eastern Arabia

reason: tribal expansion and kingdom establishment
narrative: Jafna bin Amr and his family headed for Syria, where he settled and initiated the kingdom of the Ghassanids

reason: tribal settlement
narrative: Thalabah bin Amr left his tribe for the Hijaz, and dwelt between Thalabiyah and Dhi Qar. When he gained strength, he headed for Yathrib, where he stayed

reason: Destruction of the Marib Dam forced emigration
narrative: The destruction of the Marib Dam in the 3rd century AD forced Kahlani tribes to emigrate northwards through Arabia, reaching as far as Mesopotamia and Syria prior to the 7th century Arab conquests

reason: Expansion following decline of Himyarite authority and collapse of Kindite rule
narrative: During the later sixth century, sections of Mudar expanded into northern Arabia and Mesopotamia, where the name Diyār Muḍar came to designate a distinct tribal territory

reason: Result of the Fasad War between Al al-Ghawth and Al Jadila branches
narrative: During the late 6th century Fasad War, several Jadila clans migrated from the north Arabian plains to Syria, converting to Christianity and founding a military encampment near Qinnasrin called 'Hadir Tayyi'.

reason: Islamic conquests and settlement opportunities
narrative: Descendants of Himyarites, particularly aristocratic families of Dhu'l-Kala and Dhu Asbah, migrated to Syria during early Islamic period and established Homs as center of South Arabian settlement and political power

reason: Islamic conquests
narrative: After the spread of Islam, the tribe migrated from their concentration in central and northern Najd across the Arabian Peninsula, then spread to areas ruled by subsequent caliphates

reason: Joined Muslim forces after ban was lifted by Caliph Umar
narrative: Members settled in garrison towns such as al-Kufa

reason: Islamic conquests and military campaigns
narrative: Mahri forces participated in Islamic conquests under Amr ibn al-As, establishing settlements in conquered territories including lands in Fustat (Hayy Al-Mehri quarter)

reason: Islamic conquests and expansion
narrative: Following the Islamic conquests, the tribe migrated to modern-day Tunisia, Iraq, Morocco, the Khuzestan and Khorasan regions of Iran, and other parts of the Arab world, holding significant power for centuries

destination.regionId: Tunisia, Iraq, Morocco, Khuzestan and Khorasan regions of Iran

reason: Search for pastures and participation in early Islamic expansion
narrative: Early 8th century migration of some Banu Hilal to Egypt, then in 9th century to Iraq and Levant

reason: Formation of largest remaining Hawazin alliance after other clans departed
narrative: After most clans of Hawazin departed from Hejaz and Najd to the Levant, Iraq, Egypt, North Africa, and Persia, those who remained formed the largest Hawazin alliance and were named Otaibah

reason: Economic opportunities in gold mining
narrative: During the Abbasid era, many members of Bani Hanifa and related tribesmen from Bakr ibn Wa'il migrated from al-Yamama to southern Egypt, where they dominated the gold-mines of Wadi Allaqi near Aswan

reason: Settlement and expansion
narrative: According to historians al-Hamdani and Abu'l-Hasan Bayhaqi, the Anizah tribe settled in the Hijaz region, specifically in Khaybar, at the end of the 10th century AD, fighting and expelling the previous rulers.

reason: Forcible relocation by Fatimid caliph al-Aziz after their defeat
narrative: After joining Qarmatians against Fatimids, they were defeated and forcibly relocated to Upper Egypt, prohibited from crossing the Nile

reason: Dispatched by Fatimids to punish rebellious Zirids, incentivized by drought in Egypt
narrative: Major migration of 150,000-300,000 people led by Abu Zayd al-Hilali, fundamentally transforming the Maghreb through Arabization

reason: Leadership under Sheikh Mohammed Bin Shaba'an
narrative: The first major migration led by Sheikh Mohammed Bin Shaba'an in the early 13th century from their ancestral lands near Sarat Abida to Lower Najd

origin.regionId: Sarat Abida (alhirja) near Thahran Al Janoub

reason: <UNKNOWN>
narrative: A large tribe, the Awamir originated in the steppes to the north of the Hadhramut, settling in the north in a process of migration which took place over 500 years.

reason: Settlement and agricultural expansion
narrative: In the 15th century, the region of Al-Qassim in northern Nejd was being rapidly settled through migration and the majority of this activity was by members of Anizah.

reason: Tribal expansion and search for grazing lands
narrative: Early migration and establishment in Buraimi oasis area

reason: Large section left under leadership of Al Jarba
narrative: In the 17th century, a large section of Shammar left Jabal Shammar under Al Jarba leadership and settled in Iraq, reaching as far as Mosul.

reason: Large-scale tribal migration eastwards
narrative: At some point in the 17th century, the tribe began a large-scale migration eastwards into central Arabia, displacing many other Bedouin tribes in the area, such as Harb and Anizzah who were forced to move northwards.

reason: prolonged drought in the 17th century
narrative: Migrated from central Arabia through various settlements, establishing Freiha as a fortified coastal hub

destination.regionId: Eastern Arabia via Al Aflaj, Qatif, Al Ahsa to Freiha in northwestern Qatar

reason: War between Al-Awazim and the Hejaz vilayet, and the large scale famine of Najd in 1703 CE
narrative: They predominantly originated from Hejaz in western Arabia, they migrated to the eastern parts of the Arabian Peninsula by the year 1700 CE due to conflicts with the Ottoman Hejaz vilayet and severe famine conditions.

reason: Disputes with pre-Al Qawasim local rulers
narrative: The tribe dispersed from the agriculturally rich Wadi Naqab after a series of disputes with pre-Al Qawasim local rulers, settling in various locations including Khor Fakkan, Kalba, Dibba, Fahlain, Khatt, and Daftah

destination.regionId: Various settlements in Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah

reason: territorial expansion
narrative: Settled all along the East Coast from Kalba to Dibba, as well as in the Wadi Ham and Jiri plain, becoming dominant in the Shamailiyah region

reason: Seasonal pastoral nomadism - autumn migration south for grazing and trade
narrative: Historically the bedouin semi-nomads of Albu Salih would migrate south into the desert annually during autumn, camping and herding livestock in Northeastern Arabia. They moved through Wadi Batin valley into Kuwait, reaching the Arabian Gulf coast, and traded with local villages and towns. By spring's end they would return north for summer. This pattern continued until the 1940s-1950s when most settled permanently.

origin.regionId: Albu Salih territory in southern Iraq (marshlands)

destination.regionId: Northeastern Arabia (Southern Iraq, Northern and Western Kuwait)

reason: Political expansion and governance
narrative: Migration from Oman to establish rule over East African coastal areas, particularly Mombasa

reason: Settlement and establishment of power base
narrative: The House of Al Bu Falah, which held the most power in the confederation, settled with the Bani Yas clans together in the Liwa oasis around 1700

reason: military skirmishes with Al Musallam tribe under Ottoman support
narrative: Departed after conflicts with dominant local tribe

reason: expelled by Ottomans for preying on caravans and trading ships
narrative: Established government under Al-Sabah family in Kuwait

reason: Agricultural expansion and acquisition of Naqbiyin lands
narrative: Habus gradually expanded from their original holdings on Jebel Hibs to acquire fertile agricultural lands in Wadi Naqab and other wadis

destination.regionId: Wadi Naqab and other Hajar Mountain wadis

reason: establishment of Al Qawasim rule
narrative: The Al Bu Muhair first settled in Ras Al Khaimah from Abu Dhabi with the establishment of Al Qawasim rule in the 1750s

reason: Seasonal migration and territorial expansion
narrative: The first Anizah tribe to enter Syria was the Hasana in the mid-18th century, followed by Wuld Ali in Hawran, then Ruwallah in early 19th century. The Fad'an and Sba'a entered central Syrian plains in early 19th century.

reason: Al Jalahma and Al Khalifa clans seeking new opportunities
narrative: Left Al-Sabah as sole proprietors of Kuwait while other clans established free-trade port at Zubarah

reason: Discovery of fresh water
narrative: In 1793, the Al Bu Falah migrated to the island of Abu Dhabi due to the discovery of fresh water, settling there in 1795 and establishing Abu Dhabi town

reason: Settlement and integration with Bani Yas confederation
narrative: The tribe appears to have settled in Abu Dhabi around the turn of the 20th century, comprising some 60 houses there

reason: Settlement and adoption of oasis agriculture alongside traditional pastoralism
narrative: The Khawatir, originally a Bedouin tribe whose dar stretched from Buraimi north to the Jiri Plain, gradually settled around the Buraimi oasis while maintaining their pastoral activities

origin.regionId: Traditional Bedouin territories between Buraimi and Jiri Plain

reason: Expansion and settlement in Sharjah dependency
narrative: Some Khawatir settled in the Sharjah dependency of Dhaid, where they had uneasy relations with Bani Qitab neighbors

reason: Economic opportunities and tribal disputes
narrative: Migration of Na'im families to Qatar and Bahrain in earlier generations

reason: Temporary displacement from Dhahirah
narrative: The Al Bu Shamis are believed to have settled along the coast after being temporarily displaced from Dhahirah in the early 19th century

reason: Political expansion and conquest
narrative: Sheikh Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi moved from the interior to establish control over the coastal settlement of Ajman

reason: Settlement expansion
narrative: In 1835, members of the Bani Yas settled Khor Al Adaid in southeastern Qatar
A further contingent of the tribe left Dubai for Sharjah in 1852

reason: Seeking new grazing lands
narrative: At the turn of the 20th century, the bedouin sections moved from their original grazing lands of Ranyah and Kurmah to roam the areas of central Nejd around Riyadh, along with the closely related family of the Suhool.

origin.regionId: Ranyah and Kurmah region on the border between Nejd and 'Asir

reason: Territorial expansion
narrative: Some sections moved further north, where they later established the town of Rumah.

reason: transition from nomadic to settled life
narrative: Most of Ajman left their nomadic life and settled in northeastern Saudi Arabia. Nearly all the Ajman have abandoned nomadic life and have settled in the Persian Gulf states, particularly in the Eastern Province of Saudi Arabia.

destination.regionId: northeastern Saudi Arabia and Persian Gulf states

reason: Defeat by Ibn Saud forces
narrative: Following Al Rashid's defeat in the Conquest of Ha'il in 1921, many Shammar fled to Syria and Iraq.

reason: Opposition to British overthrow of Sheikh Isa ibn Ali Al Khalifa in favour of his son Hamad bin Isa Al Khalifa
narrative: Virtually all members of the tribe left Bahrain for Dammam, Saudi Arabia after suspecting that the new ruler would attempt to tighten his control over them with British support. They were officially allowed to return in April 1927.

reason: Ibn Saud's conquest and road security policies
narrative: Around 1926, Emir Ibn Saud conquered a large portion of Arabia and allowed the tribe's branch in Saudi Arabia to settle in several settlements after securing roads

reason: conflict with the Duru
narrative: In the 1940s, the ongoing conflict with the Duru led the headman of the Bedouin Awamir, Salim bin Hamad bin Rakkad, to lead his people to Al Hasa in 1943. They returned in 1948, but in subsequent years were constantly drawn back to Hasa.

reason: Unknown - left traditional settlement area
narrative: The Maraziq population of the area had left the area by the early 1950s and are today dispersed around the UAE.

reason: Search for employment in oil and construction sectors
narrative: Large emigration during the late 1950s when many families moved to Qatar, Kuwait, and Saudi Arabia as oil exploration became mechanized and local labour demand declined

reason: Forced resettlement after Buraimi dispute
narrative: Following the Buraimi conflict, a number of Na'im were resettled in Saudi Arabia

reason: Economic opportunities and urbanization
narrative: Large migration from villages and small cities during the 1960s and 1970s in search of a better life

reason: Disputes with the Ruler of Ras Al Khaimah, Sheikh Saqr bin Mohammed Al Qasimi
narrative: The majority of the Zaab tribe accepted an offer from Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan to move to Abu Dhabi, leaving behind an almost completely abandoned village that had housed some 2,500 people. This movement resulted in the establishment of an area called Al Zaab in Abu Dhabi.
